title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
summary:
Hercule Poirot confronts Jane Hobbs regarding her specific knowledge of the murders of Harriet Sipper, Ada Gransbury, and Richard Nigges. Poirot points out a significant inconsistency in Jane's behavior: she previously claimed that justice would only be served when she was dead, making this statement at 7:45, even though the bodies at the Browns Hotel were not discovered until 8:10. Overwhelmed by guilt, Jane confesses the truth regarding a past tragedy involving Patrick and Frances Ives.

Jane reveals that while she was serving Patrick at Jesus College, Cambridge, she fell in love with him. Driven by jealousy after overhearing Patrick express affection for Nancy Dunn, Jane fabricated a lie to Harriet Sipper, claiming Patrick's words to Nancy were actually intended for the late William Dunn. Harriet spread this rumor through the village, supported by Alice and Richard, which led to the social ruin of the Ives. Although Nancy attempted to defend Patrick by clarifying there was no physical affair, the village's condemnation caused Frances to commit suicide, followed by Patrick. Jane also notes that Dr. Ambrose Flavard burned the Ives' wills to protect their reputation from the villagers' zealotry.

Following Patrick's death, Jane moved to London with the assistance of Samuel Kid. Fifteen years later, she received a letter from Richard Nigges, who had obtained her London address from Dr. Flavard. Richard visited her to ask for her help in rectifying the mistakes of the past.
